entry.title: SAP Build 中的新增功能 – 2024 年第 4 季度版本
entry.content:
更快、更智能、更高效地构建

欢迎阅读我们2024年第四季度的“产品新功能”博客系列。本文为翻译文，原文链接What’s New in SAP Build – Q4 2024 Edition，原文作者JesseSukhbayar。

SAP Build 是一款业务应用程序开发和自动化解决方案，提供了一套全面的低代码、专业代码和生成式 AI 工具。本季度，我们很高兴为大家介绍许多新版本，其中包括 AI 驱动的功能，这些功能将彻底改变您的自动化和创新能力。让我们一起来看看吧！

以下内容将为您介绍以下产品的具体亮点：

  • SAP Build Process Automation

  • SAP Build Code

  • SAP Build Apps

  • SAP Build Work Zone

SAP Build 流程自动化

创建和管理流程变体

SAP Build 流程自动化现在支持创建和管理流程变体。通过预定义的流程步骤和防护机制，流程专家和专业开发人员可以使用纯配置的方式为不同的用户群体建模不同的流程变体。通过定义变体条件和操作，工作流可以根据您的具体业务需求进行调整。借助此功能，您可以为各种场景管理定制化的工作流，而不会影响整体流程模板。

您可以在 SAP 帮助门户 中了解如何创建和管理流程变体。

在流程中通过 Actions 使用 RFC / BAPI

为了帮助您扩展 SAP S/4HANA Cloud 私有版和本地部署解决方案，您现在可以基于 RFC / BAPI 创建 Actions。通过利用 BAPI 节点库，您可以直接集成 RFC Actions，从而简化集成流程。您可以轻松搜索和浏览 RFC Actions，以找到工作流所需的必要功能，同时维护系统完整性并降低复杂性。

这一功能使您能够更高效地将 SAP S/4HANA 的功能集成到自动化流程中，从而提升业务操作的灵活性和效率。

SAP Build Code

移动开发工具包（MDK）中的生成式 AI 功能增强

移动开发工具包（MDK）的生成式 AI 功能现已增强，可以帮助您简化应用程序创建过程。开发人员可以：

  1. 生成应用程序逻辑：自动生成基于元数据的逻辑，减少手动编码工作量。

  2. 创建表单和工作流：在 AI 的协助下快速开发表单和工作流，加速开发进程。

  3. 构建强大的移动解决方案：利用 AI 减少手动操作，专注于交付具有影响力的解决方案。

通过这些功能，开发人员可以更高效地构建移动应用，同时将更多精力投入到业务价值的实现中。

了解更多详情，请阅读 @BirgitS 的博客 。

SAPUI5 开发：TypeScript 和 UI5 Linter

使用 Joule 进行 AI 驱动的 TypeScript 迁移

Joule 是集成到 SAP Build Code 中的 AI 助手，现在支持将 SAPUI5 应用程序从 JavaScript 无缝迁移到 TypeScript。TypeScript 提供了更高的开发效率，为构建 SAPUI5 应用程序提供了一种结构化且可扩展的方法。了解更多关于这一强大功能的信息，请阅读 @martinhaeuser 的博客 。

UI5 Linter v1.0 发布

SAP Build Code 推出了 UI5 Linter v1.0，这是一款旨在确保代码质量并与最佳实践保持一致的工具。此版本使开发人员能够编写更清晰、更易维护的 SAPUI5 代码，从而提升整体开发生命周期的效率。

通过这些新功能，开发人员可以更高效地构建高质量的 SAPUI5 应用程序，同时减少错误并提高代码的可维护性。

扩展 SAP Fiori 应用程序的全新体验

适用于 SAPUI5 项目的 Adaptation Editor

SAP Build Code 现在推出了 Adaptation Editor，这是一款用于扩展 SAPUI5 应用程序的新工具。Adaptation Editor 取代了 SAPUI5 Visual Editor，并在 SAP Business Application Studio 中提供，为跨环境开发提供了直观且一致的体验。开发人员可以利用这一更新后的工具，以更高的性能简化 SAPUI5 扩展。详情请参阅 @OliverGraeff 的博客 。

适应项目与扩展项目

SAPUI5 的灵活性支持适应项目（Adaptation Projects）和扩展项目（Extension Projects），使开发人员、关键用户和最终用户能够对应用程序进行定制化修改。适应项目现已成为扩展 SAPUI5 应用程序的战略性方法，提供了基于代码定制的高级工具。要了解这些项目类型的区别和优势，请参阅 这篇博客 。

通过这些新工具和方法，开发人员可以更灵活、高效地扩展和定制 SAPUI5 应用程序，满足多样化的业务需求。

SAP Build Apps

为 BTP 目标服务添加额外的 HTTP 请求

在将外部服务与 SAP Build Apps 集成时，您现在可以配置额外的 HTTP 请求头，从而获得更高的控制性和灵活性。这一功能增强了集成安全性和定制化能力。此外，新的流函数（flow function）支持使用 multipart/form-data 作为请求体类型上传二进制文件。

要了解更多详细信息，请参阅 HTTP Destination Request 流函数参考文档。

通过这些更新，开发人员可以更安全、更灵活地集成外部服务，并支持更复杂的文件上传场景。

全新的顶部导航栏

SAP Build Apps 现在提供了更新的工具栏和导航菜单，为用户带来更直观的体验。常用的功能（如“变量”）现在可以直接从工具栏访问。同时，部分标签名称进行了更新，例如：

  • “UI Canvas” 更名为 “用户界面”

  • “Data” 更名为 “集成”

此外，使用频率较低的选项（如“主题”和“导航”）被归类到 “应用设置” 中，以减少界面杂乱感。常用操作（如“预览”和“发布”）则被显著地放置在右上角，简化了版本管理和部署流程。

要了解更多详细信息，请阅读 @Dan_Wroblewski 的功能评测博客 。

通过这些改进，SAP Build Apps 的用户界面更加简洁高效，帮助开发人员更专注于应用程序的设计和开发。

SAP Build Work Zone

Joule 与 Work Zone 标准版的集成

Joule 现已集成到 SAP Build Work Zone 标准版 中，为工作区提供了 AI 驱动的强大功能。这一集成带来了以下优势：

  • 智能工作区推荐：提升工作效率。

  • 主动洞察：根据任务和优先级提供定制化建议。

  • 简化的工作流：改善协作并帮助用户更专注于关键任务。

通过 Joule 的 AI 能力，用户可以更高效地管理工作区，并获得个性化的支持。

要了解更多关于 Joule 如何改变您的工作区的示例，请阅读 @Yumnahabbasi 的博客 。

引导式流程向导

引导式流程向导（Guided Process Wizard）结合了 SAP Build Work Zone 和 SAP Build 流程自动化，简化了创建结构化、分步工作流的过程。通过这一集成，您可以在流程自动化中设计引导式流程，并将其作为向导部署到 Work Zone 中。

您可以利用动态 UI 元素（如卡片和小部件）构建可定制的工作流，从而简化复杂的入职和发票处理等任务。引导式体验功能包含在 SAP Build Work Zone 高级版 和 SAP SuccessFactors Work Zone 中。

要了解更多详细信息，请阅读 @Yumnahabbasi 的 FAQ 博客。

 

了解更多

如需查看完整的新功能列表，请访问以下资源：

  • What's New Viewer（新功能查看器）

  • SAP 帮助门户

  • SAP Road Map Explorer（ SAP 路线图探索器）

为了及时了解最新公告和创新动态，请关注 SAP Builders Community，并与社区中的其他 SAP Builders 交流互动。

entry.title: Simplify connectivity to improve developer efficiency with the help of Enterprise Automation
entry.content:
co-authored by: @EvgeniyGorbunov

Hello everyone!

 With Enterprise Automation with SAP, we bring together technologies such as SAP Signavio, SAP Integration Suite and SAP Build in order to provide a unified framework to address process analysis, automation and integration needs of an intelligent enterprise.  

These technologies have multiple integration touchpoints and can be used together for the best benefit in a vast amount of use-cases. 

You can find more detailed information about the Enterprise Automation with SAP here.

 

Practical need

In this blog post, I would like to focus on one specific usage pattern of the Enterprise Automation framework: how to ensure the developer efficiency while simplifying connectivity to underlying data sources. It is always better to explain such pattern with a practical scenario, so let us look into the manufacturing area as an example.

An organization wants a clear view of all maintenance orders and material reservations for the equipment maintenance. The bigger the company is, more the equipment it possesses – and it is vital to ensure the right maintenance timelines and see all the issues in a transparent way.

For this, the company needs to gather data from multiple sources and display it in a user interface. Later, the responsible person can review the information in a dashboard-like format and proceed with sending the relevant maintenance and repair orders.

How can they achieve their goal in a short time and with limited resources thanks to the Enterprise Automation capabilities?

How does it work?

 

Graph is a powerful enhancement to API Management, capability of SAP Integration Suite, which supports in transforming APIs. You can easily and securely provide your integration and application developers with selective, abstracted access to your enterprise data via a single, easy-to-use API. The enterprise data is semantically connected in the form of a business data graph.

To build the end user interface SAP Build Apps is being used. SAP Build Apps is a professional application development solution providing an intuitive no-code development environment to empower business users and developers to create apps quickly and visually.

 

Setup

 You can setup business data graph in your subaccount, where identity, trust and connectivity can be established between your applications, extensions and integrations, and your landscape. An advantage of Graph is that IT Administrators take care of the Business Data Graph as well as the destination’s configuration, while developers just consume the entities via Graph independent of which specific tenants they are working with.

In our use case architecture, SAP Build Apps application consumes both SAP S/4 HANA Cloud entities Reservation and maintenance Order via Graph. User Identity is propagated from SAP Build Apps to Graph, and Graph will propagate the identity to each specific backend tenant to verify the end user authentication and authorization.


Developers only connect to the Graph destination configured by IT Administrators without having to care about changes on the specific backend servers’ configuration.

 

 

 

Creation of Graph custom entity

 You can start by defining a custom entity using Graph Model extension editor in SAP Integration Suite. Reservation and maintenance order entities can be composed using the step-by-step business data graph extension wizard. The activated business data graph API will be binded to SAP build apps for further consumption.

Please refer the blog post to understand how a model extension is created.

 

 

 Creating an app with SAP Build Apps

Create a web and mobile application in SAP Build apps as per the custom scenario by consuming the data from Graph unified API.

 

Once the Graph API is consumed, it makes it possible to execute queries by consuming data  from multiple entities composed in Graph in a same UI Component.

 

 

Now that we are done with configurations and designing the UI for the scenario, our scenario would look like the one recorded in the video here.

 

 

Summary:

We saw how you can connect your applications using a unified API that abstracts underlying data sources using the Graph functionality of SAP Integration Suite. This way, you can consolidate the data, expose and easily reuse it in your application at the runtime.

But that is not all. We looked at an example with an application above. On top of that, we also face the need to have an abstraction layer to connect data to the process flow, created in SAP Build Process Automation. We can ensure API connectivity from the process using actions to encapsulate API calls, and by using Graph we can simplify this connectivity by having one unified layer underneath. If we put it in frame of a scenario above, if we need an additional approval step to coordinate, we can leverage SAP Build Apps, SAP Build Process Automation and Graph in one unified scenario.

Find more detailed information about the Enterprise Automation with SAP and how to get started in the blog post.

Find more information about Graph in SAP Integration Suite in this blog post series.

And use the community page of SAP Integration Suite for more information and exchange on the SAP Graph topic.

entry.title: New SAP Discovery Center Mission: Mastering SAP S/4HANA Extension with SAP Build
entry.content:
From the very beginning, SAP Build has been introduced as a unified low-code platform, enabling developers of all skill levels to create, extend, and automate business applications with drag-and-drop simplicity. Later, its capabilities were expanded with the integration of Java and JavaScript development, enhanced by AI features. And now the latest enhancements of SAP Build include ABAP development capabilities, advanced generative AI features, and deeper integration with SAP S/4HANA and other business applications.
 
SAP Build simplifies extension development and accelerates ERP modernization by seamlessly connecting all SAP development tools — ABAP, Java, JavaScript, and low-code — to enable effortless collaboration across diverse skill sets.
 
Start the new mission Mastering SAP S/4HANA Extension with SAP Build to try out the power of the Fusion Development experience provided by SAP Build.
 

Who is the mission made for?

 
No matter if you are a professional developer or a citizen developer with small development experience, this mission will guide you to the process of developing your extension application within a few hours.

What is it about?

 
The tutorial scenario is based on the following story:
 
Suzan, a medical technician at SBA Medical Clinic, noticed a small issue with one of the medical devices during her routine equipment checks. To address it quickly, she used the Service Hub Client mobile app to report the problem directly from her smartphone. The app, powered by SAP's Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP), immediately logged the incident in a central system, ensuring all service details were captured in one place.
 

 

Alex, the service manager at Health Tech Devices, received the report through the Service Hub Admin interface. He quickly reviewed the issue, prioritized it, and assigned a technician to fix the problem. Using the ABAP RESTful Application Programming model (RAP) power in background, Alex is able to assign the nearest processor of the issue, seeing all available technicians on the map within a selected range.
 

 

What will I do?

 
The final solution consists of several services and applications driven by SAP BTP.
 
 
You will start development by creating and deploying a CAP application using SAP Build Code. This application will be the heart of the project, processing data for equipment maintenance issues. All the steps will be done using the power of artificial intelligence provided by Joule. You will design a data model, expose it with the services, implement some custom logic and deploy to the Cloud Foundry environment, including SAP HANA artifacts.
 
 
You will then create another background service using SAP BTP ABAP environment. This will provide the data for the available incident processors. The data includes some processor information and geo-location. The main data of a processor is assumed to come from an SAP S/4HANA Cloud system (Business Partner data). For simplicity, this data is uploaded only once. In real production scenarios, there should be a more comprehensive integration. To reproduce this scenario, you don't need an access to a real SAP S/4HANA Cloud system as you will use the SAP Business Accelerator Hub Sandbox. In addition, you will create a service using the ABAP RESTful Application Programming model to search for the processors within a given distance range using the power of the SAP HANA database geo-engine.
 
 
To access the data from your front-end applications, you will create SAP BTP destinations.
 
When all background services are available you will use SAP Build Apps to create 2 cross-platform applications. You will use all the power of SAP Build Apps hidden behind the drag-and-drop simplicity, including advanced components, complex (but still intuitive) formulas, REST-based integration for non-standard data requests, and so on.
 

How can I start?

 
Try out the mission in SAP Discovery Center for free. The content of the mission is tested on a regular basis and will be kept up to date by integrating the latest services and features.
 
Even though this mission involves numerous services and applications, you can complete all the steps from start to finish using the SAP BTP Trial Account.
 
You are interested in more end-to-end missions? Pick up one of them from SAP Discovery Center.
 
We are excited to hear your feedback about this mission. Please feel free share your thoughts in the comment section below or reach out to us via Discovery Center support tool.

entry.title: The secret to better "looking" tasks in SAP Build Process Automation: Customizing My Inbox
entry.content:
When working with workflow applications, having access to the right information at the right time is critical. The My Inbox app in SAP Build Process Automation is a key tool for managing and interacting with workflow tasks.

While many are familiar with adding custom parameters in classic workflow scenarios using SAP Business Application Studio, fewer know that similar enhancements are possible in SAP Build Process Automation. 

 

Yes, you can add custom attributes to tasks in the My Inbox app in SAP Build Process Automation. This blog will guide you through the steps to achieve that, covering everything from syntax to UI customization. But before diving into the technical steps, let’s take a moment to understand the business value of this enhancement. 

Business Value of Adding Custom Attributes

Customizing the My Inbox app to display additional attributes isn’t just about improving the appearance—it brings significant business benefits: 

Faster Task Identification: 

By showing key information directly in the task list, users can quickly identify the task they need without having to open each one individually. This reduces time spent searching through tasks and increases productivity. 

Enhanced Search Capabilities: 

When custom attributes are added, they become searchable through the My Inbox app’s search box. This means users can find specific tasks based on those attributes without needing to manually sift through task details. Without custom attributes, only standard fields are searchable, limiting the ability to find tasks efficiently. Additionally, if you turn on My Inbox Expert view, those attributes will appear as columns. 

Better User Experience: 

Providing more information at a glance makes the My Inbox app more intuitive and user-friendly. Users feel more in control of their tasks, leading to a smoother workflow experience and improved satisfaction :-).

Step 1: Adding Custom Attributes in the Standard Form

To display additional attributes in the My Inbox app, you first need to define them within your form. This requires the correct syntax for adding custom parameters. This is also described in documentation.

  1. Open your project in SAP Build Process Automation. 

  2. Navigate to the form associated with your task. 

  3. Add the custom attributes following bellow syntax.

     

     

Step 2: Creating a Local Copy of the My Inbox App in SAP Build Work Zone 

Once your Build project is updated to include custom attributes, the next step is to enable their display in the My Inbox app. 

  1. Access the SAP Build Work Zone. 

  2. Create a local copy of the standard My Inbox app by cloning it.

  3. Edit the configuration of your local My Inbox app. Look for the parameter showAdditionalAttributes in the app settings.

  4. Set the parameter showAdditionalAttributes to true to allow custom attributes to be displayed.   

     

Step 3: Verifying the Custom Attributes Display 

  1. Open a task in the app.

  2. You should now see the additional custom attributes displayed alongside standard task details. 

     

If everything is configured correctly, the My Inbox app will now display your custom parameters, making it easier for users to access all necessary information at a glance.   

Continual Improvements in SAP Build Process Automation 

We’re glad to see that SAP is continually adding options previously available in Business Application Studio into SAP Build Process Automation. This ensures a smooth transition for developers familiar with the classic environment while introducing new possibilities for enhanced workflow customization.

We look forward to seeing even more features and capabilities added in future updates!

entry.title: NexGen AI Agents for SAP S4 HANA
entry.content:
NVIDIA’s Jensen Huang says that - 'IT will become the HR of AI agents'. Source

Epoch

Creating AI agents begins with defining their purpose and value. Managing their lifecycle—from development to deployment—can be complex and challenging. When it comes to SAP S/4HANA, finding a ready-made solution for AI agents is rare. However, thanks to NexGen.AI, we now have SAP AI Agents that can address some of these challenges. In this blog series, I’ll share how I built an AI agent group in 2024 that sped up SAP processes and significantly reduced manual effort.

 

The Challenge 

One of the biggest hurdles we encountered daily was dealing with unstructured emails. Automating this process wasn’t straightforward because:

  1. Emails had to be monitored, categorized, and forwarded to the right team.
  2. Team members manually extracted information from emails to process them further.
  3. This created a heavy workload, particularly for email categorization and processing file attachments.
  4. Additionally, each email needed to be acknowledged with an appropriate response.

To address these issues, we identified the need for advanced automation in pre-SAP processes. Unfortunately, the SAP ecosystem offers limited solutions that integrate SAP-specific knowledge with automation capabilities.

By leveraging NexGen AI Agents and SAP Build Process automations, we developed a solution to handle incoming emails intelligently. This AI agent could automatically sort emails, extract key information, process attachments, and send accurate responses—all with minimal human intervention.

This project demonstrated how AI agents can streamline and accelerate SAP processes, saving time and reducing effort while improving overall efficiency.

 

Is the AI-Agent already here from the Movie 'Matrix'?  

The moment has arrived, and we’ve entered a quiet tussle with AI. As humans, we just can’t resist filling up empty spaces—if we see one, we’re all over it. Remember that one person who hilariously claimed ownership of the Moon? Well, I spotted a free space in AI agent integrations with SAP business processes—and, of course, I couldn’t resist jumping in to make the most of it!

Working with AI agents is a bit like having a pet dog you adore. At first, the dog is clueless about life’s purpose but is very clear about its love for food—and you love it despite its chaos. But as any dog owner knows, with daily training and patience, even the most scatterbrained pup can learn new tricks.

AI agents are no different. Start them off with small, simple tasks so they can learn quickly and perform better. By breaking down big problems into bite-sized pieces, we can train these “AI puppies” to become specialized pros, equipped with the right tools to nail every task. Bonus: no chewed-up shoes!

Now, here’s how we’ve planned to show off these AI agents and their superpowers in automating business processes like pros!

The image above provides an overview of the high-level process flow and scope. The AI agents I have used or prepared are out-of-the-box (OOB) solutions from different platforms.

Application Scopes

The model integrates with:

  • Outlook
  • Azure
  • SAP S/4HANA
  • ServiceNow

Agentic Tools in Scope

The AI agent functionalities are built using:

  • Azure Logic App
  • SAP Build 
  • LangChain Framework
  • SAP AI Core - SAP Generative AI Hub

Overview of AI Agents

The process begins with an inbound email received at your business email address.

AI Agent: Email Analyzer

This agent leverages Cognitive AI and fine-tuned large language models to analyze emails and extract key details, such as:

  • Email Intent
  • Details of Email Attachments
  • OCR Processing for Attachments

AI Agent: Business Analyst

This agent is a collection of multiple mini-agents equipped with specialized tools to perform a variety of tasks. In this scenario, the tools include:

  • SAP OData AI: Extracts user-requested information and converts it into API calls.
  • SAP Workflow Tool: Creates workflows in SAP based on the inbound email.
  • ServiceNow Integration: Directly interacts with ServiceNow APIs to extract information and provide responses.
  • RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation): Connects to a knowledge base to handle user queries effectively.

AI Agent: Email Responder

This agent uses Generative AI to craft email responses. After processing inputs from previous agents, it generates a reply that is both accurate and context-aware. With the right prompts, it achieves results that surpass traditional solutions like ChatGPT, providing seamless and efficient communication.

This streamlined approach ensures end-to-end automation for business processes, making them faster and more efficient. 

Now, let’s dive into these two key components (and sprinkle in a bit of humor):

Shared Memory

This is where the magic happens. Shared memory is like the group chat for all the agents—except it’s actually productive. Think of it as the baton in a relay race: when one agent finishes its leg, it hands over the processed info to the next agent, saying, “Your turn, buddy!” With an organized shared memory template, the whole system runs smoother than a well-trained pet dog (you know, the one that finally stops chewing your shoes and starts fetching them).

Processing Logs

Now, logs might sound boring, but trust me, they’re the unsung heroes here. Imagine having a diary where every decision the LLM agent makes is recorded—complete with timestamps and all the juicy details. The best way to do this? Build a CAP application and store logs periodically. Not only does this give you confidence (“Hey, we’ve got receipts for that decision!”), but it also saves you when things go sideways. If the AI messes up, you’ve got a central “oops archive” to figure out what went wrong and fix it.

Revisiting the Steps:

1. New Email Arrival
A new email lands in your Outlook or Gmail inbox, triggering the event listener. This fires off a chain reaction to kickstart the process.

2. Email Analyzer Agent
The first stop in the chain is the Email Analyzer agent. It extracts all the relevant details from the email—like intent, attachments, or critical data—and passes this information to the next agent, the Business Analyst.

3. Business Analyst Agent
This is the MVP of the process. The Business Analyst agent takes the extracted information (a mix of structured and semi-structured input) and applies a “Chain of Thought” approach to generate outputs tailored to the input. Whether it’s interacting with your ERP systems like SAP S/4HANA or ECC, or connecting with ServiceNow and similar tools, this agent gets the job done.

4. Email Responder Agent
Finally, we reach the closing act. The Email Responder agent steps in to craft a polished response, ensuring the process ends with a well-written reply.

 

Is this the End of B2B APIs?

AI agents and event-driven systems aren’t replacing B2B APIs—they’re changing how we use them. APIs are essential for securely sharing data between systems, but their role is evolving. Instead of just being fixed tools for specific tasks, APIs now support smarter, AI-driven processes. Think of APIs as the foundation, while AI agents bring flexibility and intelligence. This shift doesn’t make APIs less important—it makes them more powerful and adaptable for today’s business needs.

What about this Business Analyst Agent?

Haha! AI agents can be a bit chaotic at times. As a real Business Analyst, it’s your job to keep an eye on them. These agents often struggle to figure out the next steps or process certain data correctly. Your role is to guide and channel their efforts to make them work effectively. The good news? These AI agents are here to handle the blunt, repetitive work, freeing you up to focus on solving critical business problems. While their responses are around 50–60% accurate, they’re a helpful starting point with your expertise leading the way!

 

feel free to share your thought..

entry.title: For an in-depth look at clean core and how to implement it, read our Clean Core Extensibility paper!
entry.content:
Have you ever wondered where to start to understand more about the benefits of a clean core for your ERP system and how to achieve one?

Then our new paper on Clean core extensibility for SAP S/4HANA Cloud is for you! It brings you in-depth insights into the clean core concept and its implementation, including also links to resources to move into execution.

Our aim is to allow you to extend your SAP Cloud ERP with customization as needed, while keeping the core clean, so allowing for smooth system upgrades and handling of system extensions.

Why the need for a clean core?

In today's fast-paced and ever-changing business environment, disruption across global supply chains, customer preferences, and the workforce has become a common occurrence. Businesses must quickly adapt to these changing requirements to remain relevant and thrive. At the same time, CIOs face the challenge of driving innovation and scaling efficiently while controlling costs.

Extending SAP solutions to meet specific business needs has always been a significant advantage for SAP customers. But we know that over-customization of ERP systems as it has been done in the past often leads to technical debt, making future upgrades and migration to the cloud complex.

This is an important topic for you, and we are in a continuous dialogue to evaluate best practices for ERP extensibility and to co-create the relevant technology strategy.

This is where the clean core approach comes into play: a powerful concept, model and strategy to help companies modernize their ERP systems while saving significant time and money. By adhering to the clean core principles and leveraging the powerful extensibility options provided by SAP, businesses can ensure smooth upgrades, reduce complexity, and unlock the full potential of their ERP system.

What is “clean core”?

Let’s dive a bit deeper to understand what exactly this means: An SAP S/4HANA Cloud solution has a clean core when it is up to date, documented, unmodified, consistent, efficient, and has custom extensions decoupled from SAP source code.

The clean core concept has multiple dimensions:

  • Business Processes: reduce complexity, because complex processes slow down upgrade cycles
  • Extensibility: decouple extensions from the standard
  • Data: control the data to the latest standards, with respect to volume, cleanliness, accuracy
  • Integration: keep your IT landscape reliable and flexible, using standard APIs and events
  • Operations: keep your operations effective and efficient

In this blog post, we will focus on the method for maintaining this clean core while adapting SAP solutions to meet specific business needs, which is called “clean core extensibility”. Flexibility will continue to benefit you in the future if extensions are built and utilized in adherence to clean core principles. For this, SAP provides the private cloud extensibility model for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition.

Note: The clean core concept only applies to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, as the public cloudedition cannot be customized in its source code, only extended. For the public cloud, SAP provides the public cloud extensibility modelfor SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, which also leverages the SAP BTP.

Now, back to extending SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition.

Clean core principles

Building extensions in adherence to clean core principles achieves the balance between software flexibility with customer adjustments and system stability and availability in the cloud.

The main guideline is to keep extensions strictly separate from the underlying SAP applications. Extensions must access SAP business objects only through well-defined, upgrade-stable interfaces. The clean core approach, however, results not only in faster software deployment but also in easier adoption of software changes, since the core starts off clean and is kept that way using nondisruptive, regularly scheduled upgrades.

Clean core is a framework of best practices that results in keeping both the SAP S/4HANA Cloud solution and its extensions upgrade stable. Guidelines include, but are not limited to:

You will find all the guidelines, and an introduction to the associated RISE with SAP Methodology, in the chapter 2 of our white paper.

Extend SAP S/4HANA Cloud on-stack and side-by-side

Independent of which SAP S/4HANA Cloud ERP solution you choose, be it Private Edition or Public Edition, it is standard practice to support extensibility use cases with two different architectural approaches: on-stack extensibility, for extensions that are tightly coupled with your SAP S/4HANA Cloud ERP core, which means they technically run on the same stack, and side-by-side extensibility, for extensions that are loosely coupled with your ERP core and run on a separate extensibility platform, so SAP BTP in the SAP context. Usually, a combination of on-stack and side-by-side extensibility is needed to fulfill your business requirement.

But when to use what?

In chapter 3 of our white paper, we provide guidelines and recommendations to help determine what parts of the extension should run.

  • on SAP S/4HANA as an on-stack extension, or
  • on SAP BTP as a side-by-side extension.

We present common on-stack use case patterns that will be implemented with the ABAP Cloud development model and common use case patterns for side-by-side extensions on SAP BTP.

These recommendations apply to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, and SAP S/4HANA on premise. It helps navigate the decision-making process effectively, but it is not exhaustive. It is important to analyze each extension use case and make an informed decision about which technologies work best for each case. For more information and support, please also refer to the SAP Application Extension Methodology, the SAP BTP Guidance Framework, the extension architecture guide and the SAP BTP Developer’s Guide.

Leverage SAP Build for creating extensions

The go-to place following SAP-recommended best practices to create extensions optimized for SAP software development is SAP Build. It includes

  • powerful low-code, pro-code (ABAP, Java, JavaScript), and generative AI capabilities
  • enhanced interoperability with SAP S/4HANA

to empower developers and business experts to create extensions for SAP software with greater efficiency. It allows you to accelerate ERP modernization, foster innovation, and automate processes – all within a single comprehensive suite of solutions.

The Extensibility Wizard: Streamline extension of SAP S/4HANA directly from your business apps

The Extensibility Wizard is an embedded experience in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition allowing key users to extend business applications – both on stack and side by side on SAP BTP – directly out of the ERP without losing context. This wizard can be accessed from any extensible SAP S/4HANA app by clicking the "Create Extension" option available in the user menu, on top right of the screen:

For more details on how to set up the Extensibility Wizard and what extension options are available, please read this blog post: “SAP Build Extensibility Wizard for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition”.

Accelerate extension of SAP S/4HANA with AI capabilities enabled by Joule

New levels of ERP efficiency can be unlocked with the latest AI capabilities enabled by Joule in SAP Build. Joule is SAP’s AI copilot offering conversational interactions in the respective business context. Uniquely trained on SAP data and processes, it helps developers write code and design workflows for SAP S/4HANA across ABAP, Java, JavaScript, and low-code tools. As an integral part of SAP Build, Joule is the copilot for SAP development and makes developers more efficient with their development tasks for SAP S/4HANA.

Joule generates high-quality code and code explanations aligned with SAP’s programming models, thereby reducing development time for both new and experienced developers and accelerating migration to cloud ERP with a clean core.

AI capabilities enabled by Joule can also be used to extend ERP through low-code application development. Joule assists with process automation, expediting the creation of workflows for SAP S/4HANA and guiding workflow approvers with automated recommendations.

An insightful customer example: Hitachi High-Tech

To illustrate the great outcomes of a Clean Core strategy, let me share with you the story of Hitachi High-Tech, a world-class manufacturer of electron microscopes, medical devices, and semiconductor manufacturing equipment. When adopting a two-tier public & private cloud ERP model for global operations, Hitachi High-Tech adopted a side-by-side approach to develop add-ons with SAP BTP outside of the ERP core.

“To keep the ERP core clean, we facilitate side-by-side development with SAP BTP in customizing and integration zones for our goal of simplified business processes” explains Takuya Sakai, General Manager, Digital Transformation Business Group.

“We previously upgraded SAP ERP version every five years>, taking about a year and a half from preparation to implementation”, says Yuri Yasuda, Manager, Corporate DX Dept. Digital Transformation Business Group. “However, we’re now able to perform major upgrades of SAP S/4HANA Cloud for our domestic group yearly and minor upgrades twice a year. We’ve also cut the time from preparation to switchover to around one month”.

Read the full Customer Success Story 

How to Get Started?

First and foremost, read our white paper on Clean core extensibility for SAP S/4HANA Cloud. The last chapter of the document details the six-step path to a clean core and links to many useful resources to deep dive:

  1. Start with the clean core concept and definition
  2. Apply the concept and create your specific clean core strategy
  3. Analyze existing custom code (brownfield only)
  4. Adopt extension guidelines and methodologies
  5. Establish clean core governance and operations
  6. Build extensions

In addition, depending of the line of business you’re involved in, read our eBook presenting how to Extend SAP S/4HANA with SAP Build with use cases by LoB, including Finance, Supply chain, Sales and service, Operations and IT.

And regarding the use of SAP Build for extending your ERP, I recommend watching the replay of our SAP Build Live online event on Clean Core extensibility with SAP Build held on October 23, where our partner LeverX shared insights from a real customer use case.

In addition, I also recommend watching the replay of some SAP TechEd sessions focused on the Extensibility topic:

  • DT200 | Clean core extensibility in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
  • DT280 | Build clean core extensions with ABAP Cloud for SAP S/4HANA Cloud
  • AD106 | Clean core extensibility of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition with ABAP Cloud
  • AD100 | Unlock SAP Build and ABAP Cloud interoperability to elevate your extensions
  • AD102 | Unlock SAP S/4HANA Cloud extensibility with SAP Build
  • AD180 | Build extensions with ABAP Cloud and Joule's ABAP developer capabilities
  • AI104 | Revolutionize ABAP development with Joule, our generative AI copilot

Finally, we have two learnings for you:

Managing Clean Core for SAP S/4HANA Cloud will present you how to evaluate and apply clean core principles to ERP so that they can maximize business process agility, reduce adaptation efforts, and accelerate innovation. It is for beginners, takes 2 hours and is free.

Practicing Clean Core Extensibility for SAP S/4HANA Cloud will show you how to implement extensions for SAP S/4HANA Cloud consistent with clean core principles and aligned with your business use cases. It is intermediate level, takes 8 hours and is free until you choose to pass an optional certification.

Now you're ready to begin your fruitful journey to achieve Clean Core!

entry.title: Overview of all GenAI Options in SAP Build Code
entry.content:
Introduction 

SAP Build Code, the pro-code offering utilizing Joule,  brings cutting-edge generative AI (GenAI) capabilities to developers across a wide array of use cases. These range from UI5 and Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) development to providing assistance on Mobile Development Kit (MDK) projects or HANA development.

Since its general availability in March 2024, which initially highlighted AI features primarily for CAP, Joule's GenAI capabilities have expanded significantly. As new features continue to be introduced, this blog post aims to summarize the current features and the variety of use cases they support. Furthermore, as the approach for the use of different functionalities may differ, we will also provide some recommendations that are specific to some features.

Before introducing the list we will dive shortly into what prompting is as well as talk about the difference between general LLM use and Joule for develompent. (Co-Author: @MarcHuber )

**Note: This blog will be updated from time to time to reflect upcoming and new features (See end of post)

What are Prompts? 

"Prompt" is a verb that means to cause, help, or instruct someone or something to do something. In information technology, "prompt" can refer to the "command prompt". 

Prompts are used in GenAI, including SAP Joule, across several topics. A prompt can be a command, input, or a piece of text provided to initiate a response or action. SAP Joule can utilize prompts as instructions to propose code. 

An example of a simple prompt is: "Create an incident management app." 

 

Importance of well-crafted Prompts in generating Code 

Generative AI models – including the ones used by SAP Joule – use prompts as a point of initiation, where they are given initial instructions or context to generate content. Artificial intelligence capabilities are especially useful for tasks that don’t need to consider things like cultural differences. So, app development is a great candidate for being supported using prompts. Effective prompt engineering implies creating well-structured, directive prompts that steer AI responses to ensure they are accurate and relevant to the context. 

The code generated by SAP Joule significantly depends on the quality of input prompts. Therefore, the precision and relevance of the output can be greatly improved by constructing high-quality prompts that draw out the intended response. Such carefully engineered prompts can notably boost user experience and task coherence. 

It is important to remember that crafting an effective prompt necessitates creativity. It is your responsibility to ensure your expected output is clearly conveyed to the model. This commonly includes comprehension of how the model perceives different prompts and the appropriate rephrasing of them.

 

Differences between general LLM use and using Joule for coding tasks inside  SAP Business Application Studio 

Prompt engineering is essential when using a general LLM to ensure good response quality. Prompt engineering methods such as Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting have been shown to achieve better results. Chain-of-Thought prompting involves guiding individuals to articulate their reasoning step-by-step, helping them clarify their thoughts and explore the reasoning behind their conclusions. This technique encourages deeper cognitive engagement and can enhance problem-solving and decision-making skills by making the thought process explicit. An example of this could be adding the following to the prompt:

“[prompt question]. Please break down your thought process step-by-step.” 

Joule in SAP Build Code, however, is specialized in supporting pro-code developers in coding tasks. It provides predefined slash commands for a variety of use cases, such as UI5, Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP), Mobile, and HANA development. Due to the specialization of Joule, the prompt will already be matched and improved, thus reducing the manual effort to make use of extensive prompt engineering methods. 

This chapter will further depict best practices for using Joule in SAP Build Code. 

 

Be clear, specific, and directive  

Generally, in prompt engineering it is always good to provide more context while being clear on what you want. The more precise information the prompt contains, the better Joule will understand what you really want to achieve. Example:  

  • Vague Prompt: "I need a purchase order tracking application."  
  • Improved Prompt: "Develop a purchase order tracking application where entity 'Purchase Order' is associated to entity 'Supplier'. The 'Purchase Order' consists of the following fields: ID, Quantity, Price. Entity 'Supplier' consist of the fields  ID, Name, Contact Person, Mail Address"  

It should be noted that, instead of adding more and more complexity and context into one prompt where Joule for example is used to generate multiple files (e.g. for UI5 development), in some cases breaking complex tasks into simpler ones may be a better approach.  

 

Provide Examples 

Additionally, examples provide meaningful information on how the output should be structured or content should be generated. It can be extremely helpful in some scenarios, for example in sample data generation: 

  • Before: "Create meaningful sample data for the 'Purchase Order' and 'Supplier' entities.” 
  • Improved Prompt: "Create a set of meaningful sample data for the 'Purchase Order' and 'Supplier' entities. The purchase order data should be targeted at German customers and must use the euro currency symbol (€) and the German abbreviation for quantity units (e.g., 'STK' for Stück). Each Purchase Order ID should be exactly 6 digits long. Include at least 20 records. Example format: Purchase Order - ID: 000109; Quantity: 10 STK; Price: 45,25€."

 

Use of context-awareness 

Joule in SAP Build Code in general is context aware of the project you created in the SAP Build Lobby. Depending on the project type (Full-Stack, Fiori, Mobile), certain slash commands will be suggested. Besides that, Joule can make use of files in the project. Depending on the slash command and the prompt description, only required files are considered in the prompt, thereby eliminating the need to use more input data (code) than necessary for the response generation.  

Furthermore, you can make use of the #-sign in a prompt to include files which should be taken into account by Joule. This feature use will be explained in more detail in the following sections.

 

GenAI Options in SAP Build Code 

Joule offers specific skills for SAP app development with SAP Build Code which can be requested using slash commands (“/”). As each slash command serves a different purpose, the required input as well as the output format from within Joule can vary. Therefore, there are best practices for some of the tasks, which will be highlighted in this section. 

 

CAP 

Joule offers skills for the generation and development of CAP. The following slash commands are suggested when creating a Full-Stack Application Project:

/cap-gen-app

For the purpose of creating a CAP application, which involves different parts such as cds model, sample data, and application logic, this command along with a prompt stating these requirements can be triggered. Joule will then support in refining the different parts and generate the whole application at the end.

The prompt can either be precise and context-rich or one can also start with a more vague prompt, since Joule will give guidance. An example for the latter could be:

  • Example: “Create an incident management application with Customers, Indicents, ReplacementItems, and Products. Provide some meaningful sample date.When an incident title containts the word 'urgent' then automatically add logic to set the incident priority to high. ” 

The results looks as follows:

In the sample above Joule splits the app creation process up into multiple tasks and generated some suggestions for each prompt. This serves as an entry point to then further adapt each prompt with the edit button, depending on the requirements. The play button then subsequently gets enabled and triggers the generation of the code, which can be viewed as staging files. This has to be done for all steps. If satisfactory, the application code can be accepted and all the artefacts will be created in the project. All done within the intial slash command. It should be noted, that this advanced application generation process is only triggered when providing  additional information besides the model data, such as sample data or application logic. For further information on this new feature, check out this blog post. 

 

/cap-gen-model 

Besides the multi-agent support from the abovementioned /cap-gen-app command, /cap-gen-model can be utilizied for the single purpose of creating a data model. Joule will create a CAP model along with some sample data based on the prompt. It is recommended to be clear and precise while providing a good amount of context. The following example highlights how being more specific and providing more context can result in better results. 

  • Example: “ I would like to create an incident management application. Can you do that for me?” 

This may provide already some good looking results. However, as in most cases requirements already exist for the entities, its associations, and fields, being more specific can provide the desired outcomes.

With a more specific prompt: 

  • Example: “Create an app where customers can report incidents. Include a 'Customer' entity with fields Customer ID, Name, Email, and Phone Number. Each 'Incident' should include Incident ID, Description, DateReported (Date), and Status. Each incident also includes replacement items, and each replacement item is linked to a product. The 'ReplacementItem' entity should have fields Replacement ID, Quantity , and ReplacementDate (Date). The 'Product' entity should have fields Product ID, Product Name, Category, and ExpiryDate (Date). Use associations instead of compositions for the relationships between entities.” 

In this example, the named entities are defined with the properties coming from requirements. Providing comprehensive descriptions of entities and associations ensures that Joule closely adheres to the application's context during creation. Since Joule prioritizes the creation of compositions over associations, it is advisable to explicitly specify the use of associations as illustrated in the example above. 

If the generated data from the data model does not meet expectations, there is the option to either regenerate the entire dataset to obtain a potentially more suitable outcome, or preferably, use the /cap-edit-model command to make adjustments. 

 

/cap-edit-model 

By using this command, you can edit an existing data model: 

As displayed in the screenshot, a context variable must be entered with the #-sign, so that Joule knows which data model to modify.  

But it is more than just editing an existing data model. You can do code reviews, add comments and much more. You can easily ask like “Does this code follow the best pratices of CAP?” 

You can find more examples in this blog .

 

/cap-gen-data and /cap-edit-data 

Sample data from the CAP app generation can be generated to match realistic values. For general sample data, a vaguer prompt can be used: 

  • Example: “Create realistic values for the sample data” 

Of course, with more precise information, the data will be matched better to the desired format: 

  • Example: “Create realistic values for the sample data. For the Product Entity, make sure the `criticality` values are in the range of [1(Negative), 2(Critical), 3(Positive)]. [...]” 

For further editing the created sample data or adapting existing sample data of a specific entity, /cap-edit-data slash command should be used. A context variable referencing the csv file with existing sample data has to be provided. 

To ensure the generated data meets expectations, it is crucial to use clear and precise prompts. For instance, indicating specific ranges, formats, or patterns can help tailor the output to match the desired criteria. Do not include any detail about the application logic in the data model as it will be ignored by Joule. 

 

/cap-app-logic 

A further pivotal command in the CAP suite is the /cap-app-logic command, which enables the creation and modification of application logic. This command requires a context variable and an application logic file, serving as prerequisites. For more intricate application logic, breaking down the task into smaller, manageable modifications is advisable. 

 

/cap-unit-test 

To create unit tests, this command should be executed after the application logic has been implemented. Considering the context variable of the application logic file, the provided sample prompt for generating a unit test may already be adequate. If additional test scenarios or modifications are necessary, the slash command can be invoked again to make the required adjustments. For unit tests, provide the appropriate data for positive and negative cases to make sure the tests pass accordingly. 

 

Fiori/UI5 

This section describes the use of slash commands for Fiori development, which can be UI5 freestyle apps, Fiori Elements apps, or a mixture between these two using the flexible programming model. 

/fiori-gen-spec-app 

In addition to /cap-gen-app, the /fiori-gen-spec-app slash command facilitates the rapid creation of SAP Fiori applications by translating business requirements into a CAP project with a SAP Fiori elements application. This command leverages the GenAI capabilities to interpret text or image-based inputs, defining entities, associations, and UI layouts based on your specifications. It's designed to aid developers by generating list reports, object pages, and form entry object pages, all while supporting draft-enabled applications by default. 

Since this creates a whole application, it is suggested to provide enough and precise context of the business requirements to the prompt to ensure accurate generation and thus avoid regenerating the whole project. This is also important as regenerating the project will create a completely new project without being able to take over specific parts. Here, Joule also supports an image/sketch as an input.

  • Example (Text):

 

“ I want to create an SAP Fiori application that shows my customers with their contracts. Each customer has the following attributes: 
1. Name 
2. Identification Number 
3. Address 
4. Contract Information 
5. A contract is referencing a customer. One customer can have multiple 
  contracts. Attributes of a contract include: 
6. Contract ID: A unique identifier for the contract 
7. Customer: Information about the customer involved 
8. Contract Type: The type of contract (sales, service, employment) 
9. Start Date: The date on which the contract becomes valid 
10. End Date: The date on which the contract expires (if applicable) 
11. Status: The current status of the contract (draft, active, expired, terminated, etc.) 

When starting the CAP application I first want to see a list of customers. 
When selecting a specific row in that list, I want to see to a page with details of that customer. 

It should show the following sections: 
- section "Customer Info" displaying the attributes of the selected customer 
- section "Contracts" showing a list of all contracts for this customer 

When selecting a specific row in the contracts list, I want to see a page with details of that contract. This page should show two sections: 

- section "Contract Info" containing: 
 - contract ID 
 - Customer Name 
 - Contract Type 
 - Status 
- section "Validity" containing 
 - Start Date 
 - End Date “

 

  • Example (Image):  

For more information, you can refer to the documentation of this slash command. 

 

/ui5 

The /ui5 slash command offers a broad support for many of the topics resolving around UI5 development. For example, it can be used to create and modify files in a freestyle project. This can range from adding controls and application logic to helping with configuration of the project. Furthermore, if asked, Joule can help understand UI5 related artifacts. Besides freestyle development, it also provides help understand Fiori elements and other UI5 related applications. Being clear and precise is recommended for using this command when generating code. It will try to automatically figure out which files in the project are needed as context and include these. At this stage, however, it does not know anything about e.g. the metadata of a remote service. 

  • Example: “Create a table for the main view based on the entity set ‘Products’ of the OData service, which is represented in the local metadata file. Add the following three columns and bindings to the table: ProductID, Name, Quantity.” 
  • Example: “Add a column to the table in the main view which displays a button called ‘Order’. If the user presses the button, show a message toast with the message ‘Order clicked’” 
  • Example: “What does the code in the main controller do?” 
  • Example: “How can I configure an OData service to be used in the app?” 

Furthermore, the prompt can also consider the currently selected code. For example, if a function inside a controller file is selected, the user can simply ask Joule to explain that code without the need to specify the file in the prompt. 

You can refer to this blog on how to get started with UI5 freestyle development using Joule.

/ui5-typescript 

The SAPUI5 TypeScript assistant can be used for converting a SAPUI5 application project (or single files in it) to TypeScript. It can do both, the creation of the appropriate project setup that handles transpilation, as well as converting JavaScript files to TypeScript, which includes the required structural changes in the files, like using ECMAScript (ES) module imports and ES classes. 

The converted or added files will be listed and can be clicked to inspect a diff view of the changes before accepting them. The diff may be extensive, as such a conversion typically touches many lines. Still, this offers an opportunity to identify any unintended changes. 

A certain amount of re-work of the result may be needed, e.g. to adjust some of the generated return types of functions or to add needed typecasts. 

The functionality itself tries to convert everything inside a project at once. However, in order ut to avoid being hit by size limits and inaccuracies, it is recommended to proceed in smaller steps. For example, first convert only the project setup, but no JavaScript files. Then migrate single or few files at once, depending on their size. It is also recommended to only use this assistant for SAPUI5 application code. 

This assistant is generally available inside SAPUI5 projects. 

  • Example: “Migrate the setup of the project to TypeScript. Do not migrate JS files yet.” 
  • Example: “Migrate the Main controller to TypeScript” 
  • Example: “Migrate the current file to TypeScript” 

Further information can be found in this blog post .

 

/ui5-create-page 

The SAPUI5 Create Page assistant creates a new UI5 view with a corresponding controller and configures routing for seamless navigation to this new page. 

This command scaffolds a new XML view and JavaScript or TypeScript controller, updates the manifest.json to include the necessary routing configurations, and provides default code for controller functionality. It can also add code to existing controllers which trigger the navigation to the new page. It is ideal for quickly setting up additional screens or views in your UI5 project. 

  • Example: “Create a new page with controller and routing setup. The new page should contain an address form, navigation should be triggered by a new button in the header of the Main view.” 

 

Mobile 

For mobile app development using mobile services, Joule also offers slash commands. The prerequisite for using these slash commands is having a mobile app project. 

/mdk-gen-i18n 

This command generates i18n translation files in multiple languages, each containing key-value pairs of strings. Based on your business needs, you can create additional translation files for various languages from a source file (e.g. i18n.properties). This enables you to reach a wide audience and ensure that your application is accessible to users in diverse regions. It is recommended to provide the required languages in the prompt itself: 

  • Example: “Please generate i18n files for the following languages: German, Dutch, Italian, and French.” 
  • Example: “Please generate i18n file in German language.” 

Once accepted, you must choose whether to overwrite or merge the changes. Merge file option append any new changes to existing files. Overwrite file option completely replaces an existing file with the new changes. 

 

/mdk-gen-page  

This command generates a new page containing MDK UI controls. Also, it may also produce other related application definitions, such as Action, other associated Page, Rule or Style files, with the page. The service definition is considered when creating the page. Nonetheless, it is possible to specify the desired layout appearance: 

  • Example: “Please generate a customer list page using object table section where firstname and lastname are displayed as headline. The other labels should be empty. 
  • Example: “Please generate a page displaying customer information in a contact cell control.” 

 

/mdk-gen-action 

This command generates a new MDK action. Also, it may also produce other related application definitions, such as Page or other associated Action files, with the Action. The service definition is considered when creating the Action. 

  • Example: “Please generate an action displaying product information.” 
  • Example: “Please generate an action updating product entity.” 
  • Example: “Please generate an action to call SAP AI chat completions API.” 

Note: All the MDK related slash commands are also accessible for a mobile project created in a full-stack project. 

 

HANA

/hana-gen 

This command enables users to create SAP HANA Cloud projects by specifying requirements in natural language. The tool interprets these requirements and generates a complete project structure, including database artifacts such as tables, views, and sample data. This functionality is particularly useful for rapid prototyping and development, ensuring consistency and reducing manual effort. Example prompts: 

  • Example: “Create an inventory management project and use schema name as 'inventory'.” 
  • Example: “Create a flight management project.” 
  • Example: “Create a bookshop management project and use namespace as 'com.sap' and use schema name as 'bookshop'” 

 

/hana-modeling-help

Joule offers also support on modeling questions, such as calculation views and modeling tasks. This feature can either be triggered trough the slash command or via the BAS command palette : "SAP HANA Modeling: Modeling Help (Joule)".

Here are some example prompts:

  • Example: “How to model a calculation view in BAS?” 
  • Example: "How do I add a data source to a calculation view?"
  • Example: “How do I create an hdbtable in BAS? Please give an example.'"

 

SQL generation using natural language

For the SAP HANA Database Explorer extension in SAP Build Code it is possible to create SQL statements from natural language. The prompt has to be provided in the SQL Concsole and then the execution can be triggered via the "Run Prompt" option. During the SQL statement generation the prompt along with additional information about SAP HANA is utilizied to generate syntetically valid SQL statements. However, the output has of course to be checked and validated by the user.

 

Other Slash Commands 

/help 

The /help command displays a list of prompts available in Joule for SAP Build Code. This includes example prompts and descriptions of each prompt. 

/sap-help

Questions regarding solutions,frameworks, and other information from the SAP Help Portal can be asked from within this command. So, this powerful feature provides access to a huge knowledge source.

/code-search 

Natural language can be utilized to search for code and obtain a concise summary of its functionality. The first time a search is performed, it may take some moments for the results to appear because the workspace content needs to be indexed. The results show the files relevant to the search criteria. 

The next time a code search is performed, Joule suggests prompts based on the previous search. Any new file that has been added to the workspace is indexed for code search. 

 

Inline Code-Completion  

Inline code-completion is a feature that provides code snippet suggestions directly in the editor itself (e.g. for JavaScript) based on your typing input the the context. It will be enabled per default. To use inline code-completion effectively, you can accept a full suggestion by pressing Tab or reject it with Esc. For more control, cmd/ctrl + right arrow (→) can be used to accept suggestions word-by-word. The autocomplete suggestion considers the code around the cursor and recent files to provide a matching suggestion. Furthermore, it may be helpful to write a comment with the desired output to get an autocomplete suggestion: 

  • Example: “// Create a Message Box Popup which has three actions: Save, Draft, and Cancel” 

In the example above, after pressing enter, an autocomplete suggestion will be provided based on the comment.  

 

Final Remarks 

While utilizing GenAI functionality can enhance development efficiency, it is recommended to thoroughly review the proposed code before implementation. Although Joule strives to deliver optimal results, Language Learning Models (LLMs) are not perfect. Additionally, response generation may differ even when using identical prompts. Therefore, if the outcome is unsatisfactory, you may either regenerate the response with the same prompt or revise the prompt to achieve better results.  

For example, if a data schema has been created that mostly meets the requirements but is missing a property or definition, it may be an option to rewrite the prompt by including the provided structure from the last prompt and specifically describing what parts are missing.  

As Joule in SAP Build Code is tailored to specific use cases, a don’t is to ask questions unrelated to the slash commands. An example would be, to ask for the weather, request SAP unspecific text generation, etc. The core strength of Joule within SAP Build Code and the Business Application Studio lies in its powerful tailored capabilities, where a clear and precise prompt will help you with what you want to achieve. 

 

***History - Added features
FeatureAdded
/hana-modeling-help01/2025
SQL generation using natural language01/2025
/cap-gen-model01/2025
/cap-gen-app (Advanced app generation)01/2025
/sap-help01/2025

entry.title: AI in SAP Build - Overview and Best Practices
entry.content:
AI in SAP Build: Transforming Business Applications

Introduction

Artificial Intelligence (AI) is revolutionizing the way businesses operate, and SAP Build is at the forefront of this transformation. SAP Build integrates AI capabilities to enhance application development, process automation, and overall business efficiency. This blog post provides an overview of AI in SAP Build and shares best practices in prompt engineering to help you maximize the potential of AI tools.

AI in SAP Build

Artificial Intelligence (AI) is revolutionizing the way businesses operate, and SAP Build is at the forefront of this transformation. SAP Build integrates AI capabilities to enhance application development, process automation, and overall business efficiency. This blog post provides an overview of AI in SAP Build and shares best practices in prompt engineering to help you maximize the potential of AI tools. 

What is SAP Build? 

SAP Build provides you the flexibility to create and extend apps, automate processes and design digital workspaces with low-code, pro-code and generative AI tools. Its collaborative design allows diverse teams to build solutions faster using prebuilt solutions, AI assistance, and seamless integration with SAP applications. 

General Topic: Best Practices in Prompt Engineering 

Before we start with all the AI features in detail, we have to cover a general topic. Prompt engineering is the art of crafting effective prompts to guide AI models in generating accurate and relevant outputs. For example prompts should be clear, specific, and directive. In this blog you can find all the details about the topic: Prompt Engineering

AI in SAP Build 

Coming to AI in SAP Build. SAP Build is a comprehensive suite designed to simplify the creation and customization of business applications. By incorporating AI, SAP Build empowers users to develop intelligent applications with minimal coding. This is a collection auf all the AI Capabilities in SAP Build. 

SAP Build Code 

  • Overview of all GenAI Options in SAP Build Code

 SAP Build Apps 

  • SAP Build Apps integration with SAP AI Core  

SAP Build Work Zone

  • Joule in SAP Build Work Zone

SAP Build Process Automation (coming soon)

  • Boost Development with GenAI in SAP Build Process Automation  
  • GenAI boosting SAP Build Process Automation  

SAP Build - ABAP Environments (coming soon)

  • ABAP AI  

Joule Studio  (coming soon)

  • TechEd Session about Joule Studio 

 

Conclusion 

AI in SAP Build is transforming the way businesses develop and customize applications, making it easier to integrate intelligent features and automate processes. By following best practices in prompt engineering, you can harness the full potential of AI tools, ensuring they deliver high-quality and relevant outputs. Embrace these strategies to enhance your AI interactions and drive innovation in your business. 

This blog will be constantly updated. 

Share your experience using the AI capabilities in SAP Build in the comments!

entry.title: Connect SAP Build Apps with DMS (FREE projects/videos)
entry.content:
I've created 3 new short videos to show you how to work with DMS from within your SAP Build Apps project. I've also released a demo app (both skeleton and complete versions) that lets a user navigate through DMS, create folders, upload documents, and delete documents.  

 

Video #1: #Set Up SAP Build Apps to Work With DMS

Set up the destination to DMS, add it o your project, and create a data variable with the schema for most DMS API responses.

 

Videos #2: Call DMS from SAP Build App

Use the HTTP Destination Request flow function to list objects in the root or any folder, and navigate the DMS tree.

 

Video #3: Upload a File from Build Apps to DMS

Use the new flow function to upload binary files to DMS, as well as to delete files.

 

DMS Playlist

Learning DMS? It might help to go through the SAP Document Management Service playlist , including the 3 TechBytes on DMS Integration Option, Application Option and Repository Option.

All of these videos are based off the amazing work by Ming Kho (@mingkho), who not only developed the new HTTP Destination Request flow function that enables binary uploads, but patiently and clearly helped me understand how to use it.  

 

New HTTP Destination Request Flow Function

The new flow function lets you define multipart HTTP calls, especially so you can upload binary files, AND use a destination to define the connection details .

The flow function lets you have greater control over your HTTP calls while using a destination, and in a form i is easier to set. You can set:

  • Destination
  • HTTP method
  • Request body type – json, x-www-form-urlencoded, multipart/form-data, binary
  • Request body
  • Path (this was quite helpful to be able to provide a path here)
  • Headers

Though in my sample app I only really needed this flow function for uploading files, I used it for all my calls. Even for the other calls it helped having the Path property. 

 

Download Skeleton/Complete App

I have provided an app that lets the user navigate the DMS tree, create folders, upload documents, and delete documents.

Besides DMS, some other things you can learn from the app are:

  • Creating and triggering events
  • Conditional formatting of styles
  • Conditional formatting of icons
  • Creating variables with schema based on API response

There are 2 version of the app:

  • Skeleton App (to follow along starting from video #1)
  • Complete App, ready to use.

For the complete app to work, you may need to set the destination in the HTTP Destination Request flow functions (there are 4 of them throughout the app).

 

DMS APS

The documentation for the DMS APIs are located here:

  • SAP Document Management Service, Integration Option - CMIS
  • SAP Document Management Service, Integration Option (non-CMIS)

entry.title: SAP Developer News, January 16th, 2025
entry.content:
Podcast: https://podcast.opensap.info/sap-developers/2025/01/16/sap-developer-news-january-16th-2025/

DESCRIPTION

Upload Files from Build Apps to DMS, DOX and Other Services

  • Video #1: Set Up SAP Build Apps to Work with DMS – https://youtu.be/lhBB2mM9Nho
  • Video #2: Call DMS from SAP Build Apps – https://youtu.be/SJt_BzctwbI
  • Video #3: Upload a File from Build Apps to DMS – https://youtu.be/gIW_tHOaNuY
  • Free DMS projects: https://github.com/sap-tutorials/sap-build-apps/tree/main/projects/dms
  • Document Management Service Playlist: https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PL6RpkC85SLQCsKSvIOqMzFKW30B4TKXh-
  • Ming Kho’s blog post on Build Apps and DMS: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/sap-build-apps-upload-files-to-sap-document-management-service-dms/ba-p/13963628
  • Ming Kho’s blog post on Build Apps and DOX: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/sap-build-apps-upload-files-to-sap-document-information-extraction-dox/ba-p/13964007

CAP December 2024 Release

  • Release notes https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/releases/dec24

Joule ABAP Developer Capabilities Early Adopter Program

  • https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/campaign/3822

How to implement "readonly : update" for draft instances

  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/how-to-implement-quot-readonly-update-quot-for-draft-instances/ba-p/13981904

SAP Fiori Innovation Day & UI5 Road Map 2025

  • SAP Fiori Innovation Day Silicon Valley: https://events.sap.com/noam/sap-fiori-innovation-day-silicon-valley/en/home
  • UI5 Road Map Explorer: https://roadmaps.sap.com/board?PRODUCT=73554900100800001361&range=CURRENT-LAST#Q1%202025

CHAPTER TITLES

0:00 Intro
0:10 Upload Files from Build Apps to DMS, DOX and Other Services
1:51 CAP December 2024 Releas
5:06 Joule ABAP Developer Capabilities Early Adopter Program
6:02 How to implement "readonly : update" for draft instances
6:58 SAP Fiori Innovation Day & UI5 Road Map 2025

Transcription

[Intro] This is the SAP Developer News for January 16th, 2025.

[Daniel] SAP Build Apps has just released a new flow function that lets you upload documents to the BTP Document Management Service, the document information extraction service, or any service that requires a binary upload. Minko, a developer at SAP Build Apps, created the HTTP Destination and request flow function, which takes a multi-part request body and lets you specify a destination, both of which enable you to upload to the services. He has written two blogs, one on using it with DMS and one on using it with DMS, but I want to point out that I've just released three short videos on how to use this flow function with build apps and DMS, and I want to point out that I've just released three short videos on how to use this flow I've released an entire application that lets you navigate through a DMS repo. So it will list all the documents in the root or any folder in the DMS repo, let you upload documents, let you create folders, and let you delete folders and documents. All you need to do is attach your destination to your DMS repo. And I want to point out that we also have a playlist dedicated just to the document management service. It has videos on setting up DMS, unrelated to SAP Build, as well as how to use it with process automation, and build apps. Links to all of that content are in the description.

[DJ] While most of us were taking some time off over the holiday period recently, the CAP team has kept on working and put out another regular monthly release. December 24 of this. As usual, there are plenty of highlights, some of which I want to bring to your attention here. There are so many I've actually got a list and a read through. Got some notes. And this also sees CAP Node.js at 8.6 and CAP Java at 3.6. Okay, so what have we got? There are changes and improvements in the CDS language and compiler area, specifically For example, a completely rewritten CQN specification using TypeScript declaration files, which in turn brings about improved documentation and also some Intellisense operations that are also improved. There's a new beta feature where foreign key elements of managed associations can be explicitly annotated. Moving to the Node.js flavor of CAPs specifically, there are new compiler related life cycle events. They're also in beta. There are some enhancements to CDS Env. These enhancements relate to source formats and profile-specific endv files. And CDSQL has some enhancements. And these enhancements bring about a more robust operation in general and also some new functions for CQN object construction. In the OData arena, there are also some additions and improvements relating to the OData containment feature, and also a move to allow query option-based provision of function parameters, which some of you, I'm sure, will enjoy. In the Java flavor of CAP, there are some important changes in the area of NPM build plugin support, and also the new Calaisi plugin for the document management service is now open source. Last but not least, in the tools area, there are many enhancements to the CDS REPL. Some of you may have seen these enhancements sort of take shape over the course of the first six episodes of the art and science of CAP, a live stream series that we ran recently with the one and only Daniel Hutzl. So I'll put a link to that also in the description to this item, as well as the link to this December 2024. for Capphire release notes, but also for TypeScript developers out there, you can now just use CDS watch to start the server and it will automatically detect a TypeScript project and under the hood it will run CDSTSX for you. There's also more, even more, in this December 2024 release, but I'll leave it to you to go and check out the release notes in Capphire. Until then, see you online.

[Rich] Hey friends, Rich Heilman here. I just wanted to stop in a bit and let you know about the Joule ABAP Developer Capabilities Early Adopter Program for Customers and Partners. That's right, do you want to check out the Bleeding Edge technology helping ABAP developers become more efficient developers? If so, then go to the Influence. site and register for the early adopters program. Registration started on January 15th and will end on February 15th the same day we plan to release to customers. So again, if you are ready to get your hands dirty with AI and using Joule within ADT to help you with ABAP development, then please sign up for this excellent opportunity to try it out, give us some feedback, and make the product better for all. Thanks to ABAP developers, and we'll see you. See you soon. Bye for now.

[Sheena] You might have noticed that when a field is defined as Read Only for updates in a Draft-enabled application, it cannot be edited after an initial value is provided. Andre Fischer in his latest blog post explains how to achieve the editing of such fields in Draft using the dynamic feature control option. For this, just replace the statement read-only update with features instance in your behavior definition. Then in your behavior implementation method, verify whether an active instance of the entity already exists. If yes, the field will be made read only, however, if no active instance exists, the field will remain editable. Please note that this is applicable only when an object is being created as a draft and has not yet been persisted. Check out the details and sample code in the blog post link provided in the description.

[Nico] Hi everyone and welcome to the SAP Developer News. We have some updates in the Fiori and U5 area that we want to put your attention to. First of all, the SAP Fiori Innovation Day happens on February 5th in Silicon Valley. Definitely check it out if you're in the area. AI is going to be a hot topic, there's going to be a partner session, hands -on sessions and an outlook from the Fiori team on what's to come in the future. Check out the link to register in the future. the description of this video. And also related to Fiori, more UI5. The 2025 Roadmap is out now. Go check it out. If you're interested in UI5, there's some interesting items on there, and go follow. All right, check it out. Hope you see you soon. Bye.

entry.title: Navigating SAP BTP with the SAP BTP Guidance Framework (Live Series)
entry.content:
We're very excited to kick-off 2025 with a dedicated series to bring you closer to the SAP BTP Guidance Framework.

In each of the 3 episodes, our experts will share why the SAP BTP Guidance Framework is the only link you’ll need for any and all SAP BTP projects. Whether you are an architect, developer or administrator, the SAP BTP Guidance Framework provides dedicated information about all the relevant tools and assets in one place, outlining a clear, structured approach to help you continuously innovate with SAP BTP. 

Each live session targets a specific persona, but for a comprehensive overview, we highly recommend attending all 3 sessions.

And don't be shy! Our experts love engaging with our community and answering your questions.

Episode 1: Paving the way for architects to shine with SAP BTP 

In episode 1, we’ll focus on architects.
